description: Tajweed rule catalogue (categories, canonical colors, trigger letters) extracted from Al-Islam's TajweedRules.swift. See docs/02-tajweed.md for the full detection engine specification.

categories.literalMeaning: The laam of 'al-' assimilates into a following sun letter (which doubles).
categories.shortDescription: Lam of al- is silent before sun letters.
categories.longDescription: In connected recitation this appears most clearly in words beginning with al- followed by sun letters (ta, daal, seen, sheen, …). The laam stays in writing but is absorbed into the next consonant, which is heard with emphasis/doubling.

categories.literalMeaning: A letter written in the Uthmani script but not pronounced.
categories.shortDescription: Letter written in the script but dropped in recitation.
categories.longDescription: A practical reading aid for orthographic letters preserved in the Uthmani script while the transmitted recitation does not fully sound them (e.g. a dropped alif or yaa that remains visible for script fidelity).

categories.literalMeaning: Hamzat al-wasl — the 'connecting hamzah' — is silent when joining from a previous word.
categories.longDescription: Its role is to allow smooth word-initial pronunciation when starting, but to avoid extra breaks mid-flow when linking words. Test both states: start from the word (hear the hamzah), then connect from before it (the hamzah drops).

categories.literalMeaning: Idgham = merging; bila ghunnah = without a nasal hum.
categories.applicableLetters: When noon (ن) or tanween comes before: ل، ر
categories.shortDescription: Silent-style merge into next without nasal sound.
categories.longDescription: Unlike ghunnah-based merges, no nasal hold is maintained; the sound moves straight into the target letter (ل or ر). Timing is cleaner and shorter — avoid adding nasal color by habit.

categories.literalMeaning: Idgham = merging; bi-ghunnah = with a nasal hum.
categories.applicableLetters: When a noon sound (ن or tanween) comes before: ي، ن، م، و
categories.shortDescription: Merge into the next letter with nasal.
categories.longDescription: Merging into the next letter with a gentle nasal ghunnah. The noon or tanween blends fully into the following letter (ي ن م و) while keeping clear timing and resonance.

categories.literalMeaning: Ghunnah = the nasal hum held on noon/meem with shaddah (~2 counts).
categories.applicableLetters: Noon or meem with shaddah; also used on selected nasal merge targets
categories.longDescription: Use for an explicit noon or meem with shaddah. It is also reused for a few helper target highlights in idgham-style nasal merges so those targets stay visually connected to the same ghunnah family.

categories.literalMeaning: Ikhfaa = hiding/concealing the noon sound with a light nasal hum.
categories.applicableLetters: When a noon sound (ن or tanween) comes before lighter letters: ت، ث، ج، د، ذ، ز، س، ش، ف، ك
categories.shortDescription: Lightly hide noon or tanween with nasal.
categories.longDescription: A light concealment: the noon or tanween is partially hidden with a soft nasal ghunnah, balancing clarity and a smooth transition without fully merging.

categories.literalMeaning: Ikhfaa = hiding/concealing the noon sound with a heavy nasal hum.
categories.applicableLetters: When a noon sound (ن or tanween) comes before heavier letters: ص، ض، ط، ظ، ق
categories.shortDescription: Heavily hide noon or tanween with nasal.
categories.longDescription: A heavier concealment: the noon or tanween is partially hidden with a stronger nasal ghunnah while the tongue prepares for the next (heavy) letter.

categories.literalMeaning: Iqlaab = conversion of the noon sound into a meem before baa.
categories.applicableLetters: When noon (ن) or tanween comes before: ب (changes to meem sound)
categories.shortDescription: Noon/tanween turns to meem before baa.
categories.longDescription: The cue is the following baa: articulation shifts toward the lips with a meem-quality nasal sound before entering baa, sounding connected rather than abrupt.

categories.literalMeaning: Qalqalah = a slight echo/bounce on qutb-jad letters at sukoon or stop.
categories.applicableLetters: Letters that bounce when they have sukoon or are stopped on: ق، ط، ب، ج، د
categories.shortDescription: Qutb jad letters bounce on sukoon/stop.
categories.longDescription: A quick release, not an added harakah and not a long vowel. Strength varies by position, but keep it crisp and brief so the consonant stays clear without over-bouncing.

categories.literalMeaning: Tafkhim = heaviness; the istiʿla letters are pronounced full and deep.
categories.applicableLetters: Letters pronounced heavily (elevated tongue): خ، ص، ض، غ، ط، ق، ظ
categories.longDescription: Hear it by contrast: read the same syllable lightly, then with tafkhim, and notice the darker resonance. Strongest on istiʿla letters; keep it controlled so heaviness doesn't spread into nearby vowels.

categories.literalMeaning: Madd Tabiʿi = the natural 2-count elongation of a madd letter.
categories.applicableLetters: Occurs on madd letters: ا، و، ي (normal 2-count elongation)
categories.shortDescription: From full vowel letters: alif, waw, or yaa.
categories.longDescription: The reference madd length. Mastering its steady two-count rhythm calibrates all longer madd forms. The stretch should be even and relaxed — not clipped, not drifting longer.

categories.literalMeaning: A 2-count natural madd written with a superscript (miniature) mark.
categories.applicableLetters: Occurs on miniature madd marks: alif sagheerah, waw sagheerah, ya sagheerah
categories.shortDescription: From tiny vowel diacritics: small alif, waw, or yaa.
categories.longDescription: Tiny written signs (small alif, small waw, small yaa) carry the same two-count natural vowel sound, controlled separately so miniature Uthmani madd marks can be shown or hidden independently.

categories.literalMeaning: Stop-based madd (Aarid lis-Sukoon / Leen), lengthened only when pausing.
categories.applicableLetters: When stopping creates madd aarid lis-sukoon after ا، و، ي, or madd leen after َ + وۡ / َ + يۡ
categories.shortDescription: Stop-based madd, including aarid and leen.
categories.longDescription: Triggered by pausing at a word ending: Madd Aarid lis-Sukoon (a final letter becomes temporarily sakin after a madd letter) and Madd Leen (a sakin waaw/yaa after a fatha). 2, 4, or 6 counts; keep leen no longer than the aarid length chosen.

categories.literalMeaning: Madd Munfasil = 'separated' madd: madd at a word end before a hamzah starting the next word.
categories.applicableLetters: When madd letters (ا، و، ي) are followed by hamzah in the next word
categories.shortDescription: Madd at word end before next hamzah.
categories.longDescription: Because the hamzah is in the following word, scholars classify this separately from muttasil. Allowed lengths vary by riwayah — follow one taught pattern. This color also marks Madd Munfasil Hukmi: the vocative يَا and demonstrative هَا particles written joined to a following hamzah (e.g. يَٰٓأَيُّهَا, هَٰٓؤُلَآءِ).

categories.literalMeaning: Madd Muttasil = 'connected' madd: a madd letter + hamzah inside one word.
categories.applicableLetters: When madd letters (ا، و، ي) are followed by hamzah in the same word
categories.shortDescription: Madd letter + hamzah in one word.
categories.longDescription: Both elements sit inside one word, so this is a stronger extension than natural madd. Keep the elongation measured and stable per your riwayah so the following hamzah stays clear. (Note the يَٰٓ/هَٰٓ particle exception, which is munfasil-hukmi instead.)

categories.literalMeaning: Madd Lazim = 'necessary' madd: madd + a permanent sukoon/shaddah, fixed 6 counts.
categories.applicableLetters: When madd letters (ا، و، ي) are followed by a permanent sukoon or shaddah
categories.shortDescription: Fixed required madd due to shaddah after madd.
categories.longDescription: Fixed and not flexible like optional madds, so give it its full required length whenever encountered. Includes Lazim Harfi (muqattaʿat letter names) and Lazim Kalimi (e.g. ٱلضَّآلِّينَ). Its consistency stabilizes rhythm in longer passages.

muqattaat.note: Ash-Shura (42) is special-cased: its muqatta'at span ayahs 1 and 2.

_generator: This file is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for the tajweed rule catalogue. Edit it, then run `node scripts/generate-tajweed.mjs` to regenerate the per-language constants and docs/tajweed-rules-reference.md.
